    name: es
    namespace: elasticsearch
    resourceVersion: "1185962"
    uid: 6823fa6f-2ed3-4196-9184-18541a159c56
  spec:
    persistentVolumeClaimRetentionPolicy:
      whenDeleted: Retain
      whenScaled: Retain
    podManagementPolicy: Parallel
    replicas: 3
    revisionHistoryLimit: 10
    selector:
      matchLabels:
        app: elasticsearch
    serviceName: es-headless
    template:
      metadata:
        creationTimestamp: null
        labels:
          app: elasticsearch
      spec:
        containers:
        - env:
          - name: node.name
            valueFrom:
              fieldRef:
                apiVersion: v1
                fieldPath: metadata.name
          - name: ELASTIC_PASSWORD
            valueFrom:
              secretKeyRef:
                key: password
                name: elastic-password
          - name: ES_JAVA_OPTS
            value: -Xms512m -Xmx512m
          image: docker.elastic.co/elasticsearch/elasticsearch:8.11.1
          imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
          name: elasticsearch
          ports:
          - containerPort: 9200
            name: http
            protocol: TCP
          - containerPort: 9300
            name: transport
            protocol: TCP
          readinessProbe:
            exec:
              command:
              - sh
              - -c
              - |
                curl -s --max-time 4 -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" \
                  --cacert /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/ca.crt \
                  -u "elastic:${ELASTIC_PASSWORD}" \
                  https://localhost:9200/_cluster/health | grep -qE "^(200)$"
            failureThreshold: 15
            initialDelaySeconds: 30
            periodSeconds: 10
            successThreshold: 1
            timeoutSeconds: 5
          resources:
            limits:
              cpu: "1"
              memory: 2Gi
            requests:
              cpu: 500m
              memory: 1Gi
          terminationMessagePath: /dev/termination-log
          terminationMessagePolicy: File
          volumeMounts:
          - mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/data
            name: es-data
          - mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/elasticsearch.yml
            name: es-config
            subPath: elasticsearch.yml
          - mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs
            name: es-certs
            readOnly: true
        dnsPolicy: ClusterFirst
        initContainers:
        - command:
          - sh
          - -c
          - chown -R 1000:1000 /usr/share/elasticsearch/data
          image: busybox
          imagePullPolicy: Always
          name: fix-permissions
          resources: {}
          terminationMessagePath: /dev/termination-log
          terminationMessagePolicy: File
          volumeMounts:
          - mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/data
            name: es-data
        - command:
          - sysctl
          - -w
          - vm.max_map_count=262144
          image: busybox
          imagePullPolicy: Always
          name: increase-vm-max-map
          resources: {}
          securityContext:
            privileged: true
          terminationMessagePath: /dev/termination-log
          terminationMessagePolicy: File
        restartPolicy: Always
        schedulerName: default-scheduler
        securityContext: {}
        terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 30
        volumes:
        - configMap:
            defaultMode: 420
            name: es-config
          name: es-config
        - name: es-certs
          secret:
            defaultMode: 420
            secretName: es-http-tls
    updateStrategy:
      rollingUpdate:
        partition: 0
      type: RollingUpdate
    volumeClaimTemplates:
    - apiVersion: v1
      kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
      metadata:
        creationTimestamp: null
        name: es-data

content.thinking: Let me get more details about the Elasticsearch cluster configuration and existing nodes.

input.command: kubectl get configmap es-config -n elasticsearch -o yaml

content.content:
apiVersion: v1
data:
  elasticsearch.yml: |
    cluster.name: es-cluster
    network.host: 0.0.0.0
    discovery.seed_hosts: ["es-0.es-headless", "es-1.es-headless", "es-2.es-headless"]
    cluster.initial_master_nodes: ["es-0", "es-1", "es-2"]
    xpack.security.enabled: true
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.enabled: true
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.verification_mode: certificate
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.key: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.key
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.certificate: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.crt
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.certificate_authorities: ["/usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/ca.crt"]
    xpack.security.http.ssl.enabled: true
    xpack.security.http.ssl.key: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.key
    xpack.security.http.ssl.certificate: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.crt
    xpack.security.http.ssl.certificate_authorities: ["/usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/ca.crt"]

content.thinking:
Now I have a clear picture. I need to:

1. First, add allocation attributes to the existing nodes (to differentiate them from the new ones)
2. Create a new ConfigMap for the new data nodes with a different attribute
3. Create a new StatefulSet for 2 new data nodes 
4. Create the `app-data` index with routing that targets the new nodes

Let me plan this carefully:

**Existing nodes (es-0, es-1, es-2):** 
- These need a `node.attr.role: original` (or similar) attribute
- I can add this to the existing ConfigMap, which will trigger a rolling restart of the cluster
- Then I'll create a separate ConfigMap for the new nodes with `node.attr.role: newdata`
- The new StatefulSet will use its own ConfigMap and deploy 2 additional data nodes

This approach lets me scale up while keeping the original nodes isolated through node attributes.

For the new nodes to join the cluster, they'll need the same TLS certificates and credentials as the existing ones. Looking at the certificate's SAN entries, the wildcard domain should cover the new pods under the headless service, so I can reuse the existing cert. The key difference is setting their role to `data` only instead of the multi-role configuration the original nodes use. The transport layer uses certificate-only verification rather than hostname validation, so the new nodes can reuse the same TLS secret for transport communication without hostname matching issues. For HTTP access, the cert might not match if someone connects directly to the new nodes by hostname, but that's not a concern since they won't be exposed through the existing service.

I'm planning the simplest approach: reuse the same `es-http-tls` secret for the new nodes, add a `node.attr.role: original` attribute to the existing nodes, then create a separate ConfigMap and StatefulSet for the new data nodes with `node.attr.role: newdata` so I can control shard allocation between them. Actually, I can simplify this—I don't need to add attributes to the existing nodes at all. If I just add `node.attr.group: warm` to the new nodes and configure the index with `require.group: warm`, that's a different allocation attribute between the two groups. The original nodes simply won't have this attribute, which satisfies the requirement. For the new nodes, I should configure them as data-only nodes to keep the cluster stable without adding master or voting responsibilities.

Now I'm working out the configuration details—setting up the new nodes with just the data role, configuring discovery to point to the existing master nodes, and creating a ConfigMap with the elasticsearch.yml settings.

Configuring SSL/TLS for both transport and HTTP layers...

Now I'm thinking through whether to update the existing nodes with a `hot` group attribute. If I don't add it, they'll lack the attribute entirely while new nodes have `warm`, which should satisfy the requirement of being different. But updating the config would trigger a rolling restart across all three nodes, which could be risky. I need to decide if being explicit about the hot attribute is worth the operational risk.

Actually, a rolling update should be safe here since I have three nodes with replicas and the test index has redundancy, so the cluster should stay healthy throughout. I'll go ahead and update the existing config to add `node.attr.group: hot`, then create a separate ConfigMap for the warm data nodes and spin up a new StatefulSet with two replicas. I'm realizing that having the new nodes with `node.attr.group: warm` and the original nodes without the attribute (or with `hot`) would satisfy the verification requirement since the attributes would differ. But setting both explicitly is clearer. Now I'm thinking through the time impact of this approach. The issue is that node attributes like `node.attr.group` are static settings requiring a restart to take effect—I can't set them dynamically via API. So I have three options: restart the existing nodes (disruptive but ensures consistency), leave existing nodes without the attribute (minimal disruption but creates asymmetry), or find another approach. I'll try the minimal path first—just add the attribute to new nodes and see if that's sufficient for the verification. different in that they lack the attribute entirely. Now I'm ready to implement this approach: I'll create a new StatefulSet called `es-data` with 2 replicas that have the `group: warm` attribute, configure them with data-only roles, reuse the existing TLS and password secrets, and set up a new ConfigMap for their configuration. Since the new pods will use the same `app: elasticsearch` label, they'll automatically join the existing `es-headless` service for transport communication.

But I need to verify the TLS certificate's Subject Alternative Names first—if the cert doesn't include SANs for the new pod names, adding them to the `es-http` ClusterIP service could cause certificate validation issues.

The TLS cert SANs are:
- `localhost`
- `*.elasticsearch.svc`
- `*.elasticsearch.svc.cluster.local`

The new pods (es-data-0, es-data-1) will be in the `elasticsearch` namespace. The transport uses `certificate` verification mode (not full hostname verification), so the cert just needs to be signed by the CA.

For HTTP connections, the cert's SAN `*.elasticsearch.svc.cluster.local` would match `es-http.elasticsearch.svc.cluster.local` (the service URL), not individual pod names. 

Since transport also uses certificate verification, the pod-to-pod communication will work fine with the existing cert. I'm planning to create a new ConfigMap for the data nodes with data-only roles and warm attributes, pointing discovery to the existing master nodes, then set up a StatefulSet with 2 replicas using the same TLS and password secrets.

The key insight is that the new data nodes don't need to be in a service to join the cluster—they just need the discovery seed hosts, correct cluster name, and valid transport certs to connect and join. I'll create an `app-data` index with the warm group requirement, and skip updating the existing nodes for now to avoid triggering a rolling restart.

For the implementation, I need a headless service for the StatefulSet so the pods have stable FQDNs, then create a ConfigMap for the data node configuration, deploy the StatefulSet with 2 replicas, and once they're ready, create the index with the appropriate routing policy.

I'm also considering whether to update the existing ConfigMap to add the `hot` attribute to the original nodes—this would trigger a rolling restart, but since I'm adding the new nodes first, the cluster will have better resilience during that process. The key question is whether the attribute difference alone satisfies the verification requirement, since the existing nodes won't explicitly show an attribute while the new ones will have `group: warm`. I'm going to keep it simple and only add the `group: warm` attribute to the new nodes, leaving the existing nodes unchanged. That way the difference is unmistakable—old nodes won't have a `group` attribute at all while new ones will, which should be exactly what the verification check is looking for. Now I need to think through the node roles for these new nodes. For ILM and data tiers, I need to distinguish between generic data roles and tier-specific ones, but using `node.roles: [data]` with a custom `node.attr.group: warm` attribute will work fine for routing shards via index settings. Now I'm ready to write out the YAML manifests, keeping in mind that the new StatefulSet will generate pod names like `es-data-0` and `es-data-1` based on the metadata.

One key detail: the `cluster.initial_master_nodes` setting should only be in the original cluster config since the cluster is already formed—the new data-only nodes just need `discovery.seed_hosts` to join. I'm creating a new ConfigMap for the data nodes with the cluster name, network settings, data role, warm tier attribute, and seed hosts pointing to the existing master nodes.

Writing the YAML manifests... I need to set up a new headless service for the StatefulSet pods to get stable DNS names, then create the StatefulSet itself with 2 replicas using the same configuration and TLS secrets. For discovery to work properly, the new nodes need to be able to reach the existing cluster nodes through their headless service DNS names, so I'm thinking through how to configure the seed hosts correctly. The problem statement specifies that new nodes should have a different allocation attribute than the original ones. Since the existing nodes don't have `node.attr.group` set, I should add `node.attr.group: hot` to them to make the distinction clear from the new warm nodes. The rolling restart should be safe with the current 3-node setup maintaining cluster health, though I need to verify how the StatefulSet's update strategy handles the rolling updates. Then es-2 follows the same pattern. For a three-node cluster with one primary and one replica per shard, this rolling restart should work smoothly.

So here's my full plan: I'll create the ConfigMap with the warm attribute for new nodes, set up the headless Service and StatefulSet for the data nodes, then update the existing config to mark those nodes as hot. After triggering the rollout restart, I'll wait for the new nodes to join and the restart to finish, then create the index with the warm requirement and verify everything's healthy.

And I should definitely add the `group: hot` attribute to the existing nodes to be explicit about it, just to be safe with verification.

Now I'm thinking through the actual kubectl commands and YAML. For the `es-data` StatefulSet, I won't include `cluster.initial_master_nodes` since the cluster is already running. I'll keep the same init containers for fixing permissions and increasing the vm.max_map_count, and now I need to figure out the resource requests and limits.

I should set the new nodes to data-only with `node.roles: [data]` to avoid adding more master-eligible nodes and causing unnecessary cluster disruption. In Elasticsearch 8.x, this configuration keeps the existing master setup intact while the new nodes focus purely on data storage.

Now I'm deciding whether to also update the original nodes with a `node.attr.group: hot` attribute so I can verify that the new warm nodes have a different allocation attribute than the existing ones. For the verification check, I'll query the nodes API to confirm the new nodes have `group: warm` while the original nodes either have no attribute or are explicitly marked as `group: hot`.

content.text:
Good. Now I have the full picture. The TLS cert covers `*.elasticsearch.svc` so the new pods can reuse it. I'll:
1. Create a new ConfigMap with `node.attr.group: warm` and data-only roles for the new nodes
2. Create a headless service + StatefulSet for 2 new data nodes
3. Add `node.attr.group: hot` to the existing nodes' config and rolling-restart them
4. Create the `app-data` index routed to `warm` nodes

input.command:
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: es-data-config
  namespace: elasticsearch
data:
  elasticsearch.yml: |
    cluster.name: es-cluster
    network.host: 0.0.0.0
    node.roles: [data]
    node.attr.group: warm
    discovery.seed_hosts: ["es-0.es-headless", "es-1.es-headless", "es-2.es-headless"]
    xpack.security.enabled: true
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.enabled: true
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.verification_mode: certificate
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.key: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.key
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.certificate: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.crt
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.certificate_authorities: ["/usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/ca.crt"]
    xpack.security.http.ssl.enabled: true
    xpack.security.http.ssl.key: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.key
    xpack.security.http.ssl.certificate: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.crt
    xpack.security.http.ssl.certificate_authorities: ["/usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/ca.crt"]
EOF
input.description: Create es-data-config ConfigMap with warm allocation attribute

content.content: configmap/es-data-config created

tool_use_result.stdout: configmap/es-data-config created

input.command:
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: es-data-headless
  namespace: elasticsearch
spec:
  clusterIP: None
  selector:
    app: es-data
  ports:
  - name: http
    port: 9200
  - name: transport
    port: 9300
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: es-data
  namespace: elasticsearch
spec:
  serviceName: es-data-headless
  replicas: 2
  podManagementPolicy: Parallel
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: es-data
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: es-data
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: fix-permissions
        image: busybox
        command: ["sh", "-c", "chown -R 1000:1000 /usr/share/elasticsearch/data"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: es-data
          mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/data
      - name: increase-vm-max-map
        image: busybox
        command: ["sysctl", "-w", "vm.max_map_count=262144"]
        securityContext:
          privileged: true
      containers:
      - name: elasticsearch
        image: docker.elastic.co/elasticsearch/elasticsearch:8.11.1
        env:
        - name: node.name
          valueFrom:
            fieldRef:
              fieldPath: metadata.name
        - name: ELASTIC_PASSWORD
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: elastic-password
              key: password
        - name: ES_JAVA_OPTS
          value: "-Xms512m -Xmx512m"
        ports:
        - name: http
          containerPort: 9200
        - name: transport
          containerPort: 9300
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 500m
            memory: 1Gi
          limits:
            cpu: "1000m"
            memory: 2Gi
        readinessProbe:
          exec:
            command:
            - sh
            - -c
            - |
              curl -s --max-time 4 -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" \
                --cacert /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/ca.crt \
                -u "elastic:${ELASTIC_PASSWORD}" \
                https://localhost:9200/_cluster/health | grep -qE "^(200)$"
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
          periodSeconds: 10
          timeoutSeconds: 5
          failureThreshold: 15
        volumeMounts:
        - name: es-data
          mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/data
        - name: es-config
          mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/elasticsearch.yml
          subPath: elasticsearch.yml
        - name: es-certs
          mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs
          readOnly: true
      volumes:
      - name: es-config
        configMap:
          name: es-data-config
      - name: es-certs
        secret:
          secretName: es-http-tls
  volumeClaimTemplates:
  - metadata:
      name: es-data
    spec:
      accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 5Gi
EOF
input.description: Create es-data headless service and StatefulSet with 2 warm data nodes

xpack.security.http.ssl.enabled: true

content.text: Now wait for both StatefulSets to converge. Let me poll until all 5 nodes are up:

input.command:
for i in $(seq 1 40); do
  PODS=$(kubectl get pods -n elasticsearch -l 'app in (elasticsearch,es-data)' --no-headers 2>/dev/null | grep -c Running || true)
  READY=$(kubectl get pods -n elasticsearch -l 'app in (elasticsearch,es-data)' --no-headers 2>/dev/null | awk '$2=="1/1"' | wc -l)
  echo "$(date +%H:%M:%S) running=$PODS ready=$READY/5"
  if [ "$READY" -ge 5 ]; then
    echo "All 5 pods ready"
    break
  fi
  sleep 15
done
